---
title: "Optimization and Inference"
description: "A public-facing hub showing how the site's math modules reappear in measurement models, hidden variables, posterior questions, regularized recovery, and approximate inference."
page-type: application-index
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: optimization-and-inference
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Map the site's probability, statistics, and optimization modules to recurring inference objects."
  - "Choose a first inference-facing reading path based on current interest."
  - "See where latent variables, likelihoods, regularization, and uncertainty enter the same problem."
applications:
  - inference
  - optimization
  - estimation
  - bayesian
keywords:
  - inference
  - optimization
  - latent-variables
  - posterior
  - applications
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-26
source-check-date: 2026-04-26
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Why This Section Exists

Many readers can follow the individual math topics, but still do not have a clean picture of what an inference problem actually is.

This hub is for the moment when you want to answer questions like:

- what exactly is hidden, observed, or only partially measured?
- where do likelihoods, priors, regularizers, and posteriors all come from?
- when should a problem be solved by optimization, sequential estimation, variational approximation, or sampling?

The rule for this section is simple:

`every inference page should point back to the exact observed data, hidden variables, and approximation choices it uses`

## What Optimization And Inference Keeps Reusing

Across inverse problems, Bayesian estimation, latent-variable modeling, filtering, and active sensing, the same mathematical objects keep returning:

- observed data or measurements
- hidden states, parameters, or latent variables
- forward models and likelihoods
- priors, regularizers, or structural assumptions
- posterior questions, uncertainty summaries, or approximate surrogates

If you can identify those objects quickly, papers about inference stop feeling like separate dialects.

::: {.meta-strip}
::: {.meta-box}
**Best Starting Math**
[Probability](../../topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd), [Statistics](../../topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd), [Optimization](../../topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd)
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Best Sequential Bridge**
[Signal Processing and Estimation](../../topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/index.qmd)
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Best Uncertainty Bridge**
[Stochastic Processes](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-processes/index.qmd)
:::
:::

## Start Here By Interest

### If You Want The Shortest Math-to-Inference Entry

Start in this order:

1. [Statistics](../../topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd)
2. [Optimization](../../topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd)
3. [Measurements, Models, and Hidden Variables](measurements-models-and-hidden-variables.qmd)
4. [Likelihoods, Priors, and MAP Estimation](likelihoods-priors-and-map-estimation.qmd)
5. [Filtering, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](filtering-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)

### If You Care Most About Posterior Questions And Hidden State

Start with:

1. [Measurements, Models, and Hidden Variables](measurements-models-and-hidden-variables.qmd)
2. [Likelihoods, Priors, and MAP Estimation](likelihoods-priors-and-map-estimation.qmd)
3. [Filtering, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](filtering-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)
4. [State Estimation, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](../../topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/concepts/state-estimation-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)
5. [Partial Observability, Belief States, and RL/Control Bridges](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/partial-observability-belief-states-and-rl-control-bridges.qmd)

### If You Care Most About Approximation And Uncertainty

Start with:

1. [Measurements, Models, and Hidden Variables](measurements-models-and-hidden-variables.qmd)
2. [Likelihoods, Priors, and MAP Estimation](likelihoods-priors-and-map-estimation.qmd)
3. [Variational Inference, ELBO, and Tractable Approximation](variational-inference-elbo-and-tractable-approximation.qmd)
4. [Variational Objectives, ELBO, and Information Bounds](../../topics/advanced/information-theory/concepts/variational-objectives-elbo-and-information-bounds.qmd)
5. [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](../../topics/advanced/stochastic-processes/concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd)

## First-Pass Route

The cleanest live first-pass route in this section right now is:

1. [Measurements, Models, and Hidden Variables](measurements-models-and-hidden-variables.qmd)
2. [Likelihoods, Priors, and MAP Estimation](likelihoods-priors-and-map-estimation.qmd)
3. [Filtering, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](filtering-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)
4. [Variational Inference, ELBO, and Tractable Approximation](variational-inference-elbo-and-tractable-approximation.qmd)
5. [Sampling, Mixing, and MCMC for Inference](sampling-mixing-and-mcmc-for-inference.qmd)
6. [Bayesian Optimization, Active Sensing, and Information Gathering](bayesian-optimization-active-sensing-and-information-gathering.qmd)

Use this hub when you want the shortest translation from pure math into the recurring question:

`what hidden quantity am I trying to infer from incomplete or noisy observations, and what computational strategy should I trust?`

## How To Use This Section

- Use `Topics` when you want the math itself.
- Use `Applications > Optimization and Inference` when you want the translation layer from models and objectives into real inference tasks.
- Use [Paper Lab](../../paper-lab/index.qmd) when the inference objects feel clear and you want paper-reading practice.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [6.011 / Signals, Systems and Inference](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-011-signals-systems-and-inference-spring-2018/) - `First pass` - official MIT course showing how observation models and inference questions arise together. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [16.322 / Stochastic Estimation and Control](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-322-stochastic-estimation-and-control-fall-2004/) - `First pass` - official MIT anchor for hidden-state estimation and model-based inference. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [EE278 / Introduction to Statistical Signal Processing](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee278/) - `Second pass` - official Stanford course anchor for estimation, filtering, and inference from noisy observations. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [STATS 305B / Applied Statistics II](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/) - `Second pass` - official Stanford anchor for likelihoods, regularization, and modern statistical estimation. Checked `2026-04-26`.
- [CS238 / Decision Making Under Uncertainty](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2158402) - `Bridge to sequential inference` - useful once inference and planning begin to overlap. Checked `2026-04-26`.
